top10.introHtml: Top picks for a cheap fun second convertible within 35 miles of Media, PA — ranked by driving enjoyment, mechanical reliability, top condition, and value. All from AutoTrader page 1 (26 of 66 available listings); Chrome extension was offline so Cars.com and CarGurus local data unavailable this run.

picks.whyGood: The MR2 Spyder is arguably the purest sports car in this price range — mid-engine, lightweight (~2,200 lbs), and powered by Toyota's 1ZZ-FE which is bulletproof. At 74K miles this is a low-use example, clean title, no accidents, and the Private Seller Exchange platform adds buy-online convenience. The manual gearbox (confirm at inquiry) makes it the most engaging drive on this list. Under $10K for a running mid-engine roadster with Toyota reliability is exceptional value.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the soft top through a full open/close cycle; check the rear plastic window for yellowing or cracking. Inspect under rear storage lid for water intrusion. Look for oil leaks at the cam cover (known on 1ZZ-FE). Check for the 1ZZ oil consumption issue (look for smoke at cold startup). Confirm 5-speed manual. Inspect for rust at rocker panels and underbody — MR2s can rust if not garaged.
picks.bottomLine: Best pure sports car on the list at a reasonable price — buy it before it's gone.

picks.whyGood: The 2.8L Z3 is the sweet spot of the lineup — 189 hp inline-6 vs. the underpowered 1.9L — and at 75K miles this is a properly low-mileage example. Clean title, no accidents, very close at 14.81 miles, and asking just $7,940. The Z3 2.8 is well-regarded for driving feel, looks, and relative reliability compared to the later E85 Z4. Private seller means room to negotiate.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the soft top (check frame latches and rear window condition). Check for oil leaks around the valve cover and oil filter housing — common on M52 engine. Look for cooling system hose brittleness (replace if original). Inspect for rust in typical BMW spots: undercarriage, rear wheel arches, frame rails. Confirm manual or auto, and test all electrics (windows, HVAC).
picks.bottomLine: Best dollar-per-smile on the list — low miles, right engine, right price.

picks.whyGood: Autotrader rates it a Good Deal. The E85 Z4 with the 2.5i (184 hp inline-6) at 88K miles is a well-sorted modern roadster — better suspension geometry than the Z3, more interior refinement, and the manual top is straightforward. Close by at Mainline Auto, no accidents reported, and the price is fair for the spec.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the manual soft top through full cycle; check for tears or delamination at rear window seam. Inspect for oil leaks at valve cover and VANOS unit. Check power steering hose (early E85 known leak). Test cooling system (thermostat housing on M54 can fail). Confirm 5-speed or 6-speed manual — confirm it's not the sluggish automatic.
picks.bottomLine: Good Deal badge, good mileage, close by — a refined step up from the Z3.

picks.whyGood: 65K miles is genuinely low for a 2007 MINI Cooper S — the supercharged R52 makes 163 hp and the convertible top is a simple power soft-top. No accidents, reasonably priced at $9,500. The S trim makes this a legitimately fun driver vs. the base Cooper. The supercharger is reliable at this mileage if properly maintained.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the power soft top through full open/close; listen for motor hesitation or binding. Check supercharger coupler (R52 S known failure: the rubber coupler deteriorates — cheap fix if caught early). Inspect for coolant leaks at the thermostat housing. Check MINI's known 'death rattle' at cold start (timing chain tensioner on R52 S). Look for rust around the windshield frame.
picks.bottomLine: Low-mile supercharged fun at a fair price — check the supercharger coupler first.

picks.whyGood: The E93 328i convertible is a proper 4-seater with a proper folding hardtop — more practical than a roadster and still a great driver. 80K miles is moderate for a 2010, it's clean title via Private Seller Exchange, and it's only 14.81 miles away. The N51/N52 inline-6 is generally reliable; just keep up with cooling system maintenance.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the folding hardtop through a full open/close cycle (E93 hardtop is complex — check for binding or error messages). Inspect rear seat area for water stains (top seal failure is common). Check oil level for the N52 high-consumption issue. Inspect cooling system hoses and expansion tank. Look for rust at the convertible top frame mounting points at the rocker sills.
picks.bottomLine: Most practical convertible on the list — 4 seats, folding hardtop, manageable miles.

picks.whyGood: Newly listed, 74K miles, no accidents, sold by a Ford dealer which suggests a trade-in that passed their lot standards. The C70 T5 has a refined folding hardtop, 227 hp turbocharged 5-cylinder, and Volvo's safety reputation. At this mileage it's early in its life. The C70 is often overlooked, which keeps prices fair.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the folding hardtop through full cycle (3-piece mechanism — check for binding or warning lights). Inspect rear carpet and trunk for water intrusion. Check turbo boost under load. Look for cracked PCV hose (T5 known). Inspect for rust at lower door corners and sills — C70 can rust if not garaged in wet climates.
picks.bottomLine: Understated, newly listed, very low miles — a sleeper pick among the Volvos.

picks.whyGood: A second low-mileage Z3 on the list — this one has the 2.5i (170 hp) vs. the 2.8 above. Slightly less power but same great driving character, 75K miles, no accidents. Good alternative if the 2.8 above is gone.
picks.whatToVerify: Same Z3 checklist as rank 2: soft top condition, M52TU oil leaks, cooling hoses, rust at wheel arches and underbody. Compare this one directly against the 2.8 — the extra $2K for the 2.8 is worth it for the additional power.
picks.bottomLine: Solid backup to the Z3 2.8 — same low miles, slightly less power, same price bracket.

picks.whyGood: The Boxster is one of the best-driving cars on this entire list — mid-engine, exquisite balance, 2.7L flat-6. At 74K miles and Good Deal rating it's priced right. The IMS bearing risk on this 2001 is the key concern: if it has documented IMS bearing replacement (LN Engineering kit or similar) this climbs to #2 on the list. Clean title, close by at 11.2 miles, private seller.
picks.whatToVerify: CRITICAL: Ask for documentation of IMS bearing replacement — this is the $3K-$6K single-point-of-failure on early Boxsters. Also check for RMS (rear main seal) leak. Operate the soft top through full cycle; check micro-switch alignment. Inspect for water in footwells (top seal failure). Check for coolant leaks at water pump. Get a PPI from a Porsche-knowledgeable shop before buying.
picks.bottomLine: Best driver on the list IF the IMS is documented — make that documentation a hard requirement.

picks.whyGood: The NC Miata (2006–2015) is famous for running to 200K+ miles with routine maintenance. Grand Touring trim means heated seats, Bose audio, and the retractable hardtop option (PRHT) — confirm whether this is the hardtop or soft-top model. At $11,450 for 177K miles on a Miata it's fairly priced. The 2.0L MZR engine is rock-solid if it gets regular oil changes.
picks.whatToVerify: Confirm PRHT (retractable hardtop) or soft-top — operate it through a full cycle. Check timing chain (NC uses chain, not belt). Look for clutch wear (high-mileage Miatas often have worn clutches). Check for coolant leaks at water pump. Inspect soft-top fabric or hardtop weatherstripping for wear. PPI strongly recommended at this mileage.
picks.bottomLine: A high-mileage Miata is often better than a low-mileage anything else — if maintained, this will keep going.

picks.whyGood: Only 62K miles on a 2007 Audi A4 Cabriolet 2.0T — genuinely low for this age. No accidents. The B7 A4 Cabriolet is a proper 4-seater with a power soft-top and the 200 hp 2.0T FSI engine. West Chester at 22 miles from Media. Audi maintenance can be costly but at this mileage a well-kept example should be solid.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the power soft top (B7 Cab top motor is reliable but check for hesitation). Check for carbon buildup on intake valves (2.0T FSI known issue — walnut blast if not done). Inspect timing belt service history (B7 2.0T FSI uses a belt — critical service). Look for oil leaks at cam chain cover. Check for water intrusion in rear footwells.
picks.bottomLine: Lowest-mileage Audi on the list — check timing belt history before anything else.

mustangs.introHtml: Ford Mustang convertibles within 35 miles of Media, PA in the $7,000–$15,000 range. One SN-95 GT found in this page-1 AutoTrader sample; additional Mustangs may exist in the remaining 40 AutoTrader listings.

picks.whyGood: A 1995 SN-95 Mustang GT with only 55K miles — that mileage is remarkable for a 31-year-old pony car. No accidents reported. The GT means 5.0L V8 (215 hp), which is the whole point of a Mustang. At $10,495 it's fairly priced for the low-mileage, clean history. Manual or automatic unknown — confirm at inquiry, as manual is strongly preferred.
picks.whatToVerify: Inspect the SN-95 specific convertible-top-mount-to-rocker rust — this is a known failure point, look carefully under the rear quarter panel trim and at the rocker panel where the top mounts bolt through. Check the T-5 manual gearbox (if equipped) for third-gear synchro — slow-shift to 3rd and listen for grind. Inspect the rear 8.8 axle for clunk on takeoff. Look for typical Mustang convertible water staining on rear seat from top seal failure.
picks.bottomLine: Low-mileage SN-95 GT is a rare find — verify the rocker rust and top mount situation first.
